citation: Desai SS, Dent CS, El-Najjar DB, Swindell HW, Popkin CA. Musculoskeletal Injury in American Football: A Bibliometric Analysis of the Most Cited Articles. Orthop J Sports Med. 2023;11(6):23259671231168875. doi:10.1177/23259671231168875
abstract: This cross-sectional bibliometric study identified the 50 most cited articles on musculoskeletal injury in American football. Web of Science and Scopus were searched for football injury articles while excluding concussion, CTE, soccer, rugby, Australian football, and non-musculoskeletal topics. The most cited paper was Boytim et al.'s 1991 syndesmotic ankle sprain study with 227 citations. Most influential articles were published in the American Journal of Sports Medicine, emphasized lower extremity injuries, and were commonly level 4 evidence. The authors concluded that football musculoskeletal medicine still needs more prospective research, especially for upper extremity injuries.
tldr: The most influential football musculoskeletal injury literature is dominated by lower extremity epidemiology/outcomes and mostly lower-level evidence; staff should treat it as a reading map, not a complete evidence base.
methods: The authors searched Web of Science and Scopus through April 27, 2022 using football and injury terms, screened records by citation count, and selected the 50 most cited American football musculoskeletal injury articles. They extracted citation count and density, publication decade, journal, country, repeated authorship, topic, injury area, and Oxford level of evidence, then compared citation patterns statistically.
findings: Mean citation count was 102.76 +/- 37.11. The top-cited article was on syndesmotic ankle sprains. The American Journal of Sports Medicine published 31 of 50 articles. Epidemiology/outcomes was the most common topic, knee was the most common specific injury area, and lower extremity injuries heavily outnumbered upper extremity injuries. Twenty-eight papers were level 4 evidence, and only one was level 1.
limitations: The search intentionally excluded concussion and CTE literature, which are major parts of football health research. Bibliometric ranking can miss relevant papers, favors older publications, does not prove study quality, and does not capture influence through nonindexed journals, textbooks, talks, or digital media.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football sport science staff, this paper is useful as a curated starting point for journal clubs, injury-prevention reviews, and clinician education. It also highlights where internal surveillance and applied research can add value, particularly prospective football-specific studies and underrepresented injury areas.
athleteDev: The paper does not test athlete-development interventions directly, but it points staff toward established injury themes: knee, ankle, hip, spine, hamstring/adductor, and shoulder problems. Development plans should blend landmark literature with current monitoring, strength balance work, tissue capacity building, and position-specific exposure data.
rtp: No RTP protocol was tested. The article notes that football RTP decisions vary by sport population and competition level, so RTP planning should rely on injury-specific evidence plus athlete-specific function, workload, position demands, and medical clearance.

citation: Carrabba NV, Ong J, Vogt AZ, Panzo NJ, Memon H, Lee VA, et al. National Football League Game Officials Self-Rating of Knowledge in Neuro-Ophthalmic Principles and Practice: A Pilot Program to Improve Precision and Accuracy of Game Official Calls. J Neuroophthalmol. 2024;00:1-6. doi:10.1097/WNO.0000000000002129
abstract: PDF extraction note: the source filename names an NFL Dynamic Kickoff injury-incidence paper, but the readable PDF content is a Journal of Neuro-Ophthalmology article on NFL game officials' neuro-ophthalmic education. The study tested whether a structured curriculum on neuro-ophthalmic principles improved NFL officials' self-rated knowledge and likelihood of using terms relevant to play-calling. At the 2023 NFL Official Training Camp, 142 officials, replay officials, and officiating staff completed pre- and postlecture feedback. Self-rated understanding of saccades, pursuit, vestibulo-ocular reflex, selective attention deficit, dynamic visual acuity, and visual field improved significantly, and officials rated the curriculum and game-video examples as highly relevant.
tldr: The PDF appears mislabeled; the extracted study supports structured perceptual-decision training for NFL officials, not a Dynamic Kickoff injury analysis.
methods: A formal neuro-ophthalmic principles curriculum was delivered in person to 121 NFL game officials, 17 replay officials, and 4 officiating staff at NFL Official Training Camp in Irving, Texas on September 8-9, 2023. Participants completed anonymous hard-copy Likert feedback forms before and after the lecture/videos. Paired two-tailed t tests compared pre- and postintervention self-rated knowledge and likelihood of using neuro-ophthalmic terms.
findings: All 142 participants completed the main survey items. Understanding of saccade, pursuit, and vestibulo-ocular reflex rose from roughly 2.6-2.7 to 7.9-8.0 on a 10-point scale. Understanding of selective attention deficit, dynamic visual acuity, and visual field rose to 7.7, and likelihood of using oculomotor terms rose from 2.9 to 7.5. Curriculum and video relevance were rated about 8.8/10, with strong interest in more content.
limitations: This was a self-rating education study, not an objective test of officiating accuracy, injury outcomes, or player safety. The available NFL official cohort was small, some participants may have completed a prior pilot curriculum, anonymous surveys prevented removing those individuals, and one back-page question had lower response.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football staff, the transferable point is that shared vocabulary and video-based perceptual training may improve real-time decision quality. Similar concepts could be applied to sideline spotting, practice safety observation, return-to-play monitoring, and staff communication around line of sight, visual field, attention, and movement constraints.
athleteDev: No player-development outcomes were studied. Indirectly, better staff and official decision training can support safer environments by improving detection, communication, and positioning decisions during chaotic football actions.
rtp: No RTP data or player injury management protocol was provided in the extracted PDF content.

citation: Sirisena D, Walter J, Probert J. National Football League concussion lawsuit: what it means for other sports and observations from Singapore Rugby. Br J Sports Med. 2017;51:696-697. doi:10.1136/bjsports-2016-096565
abstract: This editorial uses the NFL concussion lawsuit and Singapore Rugby experience to discuss medicolegal and practical risks for sports that do not detect, communicate, and manage concussion adequately. The authors note the NFL settlement may set precedent for other sports, while media scrutiny increases pressure on sideline clinicians. They contrast regulated approaches such as World Rugby's Head Injury Assessment and pitchside medical training with challenges in global soccer and multilingual settings, including Head Injury Assessment translation problems in Singapore. They call for legislation, stakeholder education, clinical guidance, and evidence-based best practice across sports.
tldr: Concussion management is a governance, education, communication, and legal-risk issue; standardized protocols and stakeholder education protect athletes and staff.
methods: This was an editorial commentary rather than an empirical study. The authors synthesized legal context from the NFL concussion settlement, published concussion-incidence comparisons across youth sports, World Rugby policy experience, FIFA implementation challenges, and local observations from Singapore Rugby.
findings: The NFL lawsuit highlighted the risk of inadequate concussion warning, recognition, and management. Rugby has adopted more explicit head injury substitution, HIA tools, and medical training, while soccer faces implementation challenges across levels and countries. Singapore Rugby experience showed that language and translation can weaken assessment quality and communication of seriousness.
limitations: The article is opinion/commentary and does not provide new outcome data, effect sizes, or validated intervention testing. Its observations may not generalize across all sports systems, and it predates later consensus updates and protocol changes.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, the message is to keep concussion education current and documented for athletes, coaches, families, and staff; use standardized sideline pathways; and remove ambiguity from who can evaluate, who can clear, and how symptoms are communicated. Translation, health-literacy, and cultural barriers should be planned for before the season.
athleteDev: Athlete development should include repeated concussion literacy: symptom reporting, teammate reporting, why removal is protective, and why hiding symptoms threatens long-term availability. Coaches and performance staff need the same language so competitive pressure does not dilute clinical decisions.
rtp: No detailed RTP protocol is tested. The editorial supports cautious removal and qualified medical management after suspected concussion, with return decisions handled by clinicians using current evidence-based guidelines rather than game context or media pressure.

citation: Ellenbogen RG, Batjer H, Cardenas J, Berger M, Bailes J, Pieroth E, et al. National Football League Head, Neck and Spine Committee's Concussion Diagnosis and Management Protocol: 2017-18 season. Br J Sports Med. 2018;52:894-902. doi:10.1136/bjsports-2018-099203
abstract: This consensus statement describes the NFL Head, Neck and Spine Committee's 2017-18 concussion diagnosis and management protocol and its clinical rationale. The protocol synthesizes contemporary concussion science, including the Berlin consensus, and adapts it to NFL practice and game-day realities. It details preseason education and baseline assessment, sideline and locker-room evaluation, roles for team physicians, unaffiliated neurotrauma consultants, athletic trainer spotters, medical timeouts, serial follow-up, documentation, quality assurance, and graduated return to participation with independent neurological consultant confirmation before full return.
tldr: The NFL protocol emphasizes immediate recognition, removal when indicated, independent consultation, serial assessment, documented checklists, and a staged no-symptom progression before full football clearance.
methods: This was a consensus/protocol article, not a trial. The NFL Head, Neck and Spine Committee synthesized available evidence and international consensus guidance, then described the operational protocol used at the end of the 2017-18 NFL season, including game-day roles, checklist requirements, and return-to-participation steps.
findings: Key components include preseason education and baseline data, sideline screening for observed or reported signs, no-go criteria requiring removal to the locker room, UNC involvement as an independent co-pilot, ATC spotters with video review and medical-timeout authority, full locker-room concussion assessment, Madden Rule protections after diagnosis, next-day reassessment, mandatory game-day checklist documentation, and quality review of possible violations.
limitations: Concussion remains a clinical diagnosis; the article notes no validated real-time biomarker, imaging tool, or biomechanical technology can unequivocally diagnose sport-related concussion. Brief exams can be limited by practice effects and may appear normal despite concussion, so clinician familiarity with the athlete and serial assessment remain essential. Authors also had NFL roles or reimbursements, and the protocol requires yearly revision as evidence evolves.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, this is a strong operational template: define roles before the season, rehearse communication between medical staff and spotters, document each step, empower medical timeouts/removal, repeat evaluation before athletes leave the venue, and separate medical decisions from coaching decisions.
athleteDev: Education should normalize symptom reporting by players, teammates, and coaches. Baseline cognitive, vestibular, ocular, and symptom profiles help staff recognize subtle changes, while culture-building is needed so players do not treat reporting as weakness.
rtp: The protocol uses individualized progression with no fixed timeline. Step 1 is relative physical and cognitive rest until symptoms and neurologic exam return to baseline. Step 2 is light aerobic exercise. Step 3 increases aerobic work and introduces strength training. Step 4 adds noncontact football-specific activity. Step 5 allows full football practice/contact after symptom-free noncontact work and baseline neurocognitive testing. Step 6 requires independent neurological consultant review and face-to-face confirmation before game clearance.

citation: Haralson W, Garlapaty A, Baumann J, Jackson G, Cook JL, DeFroda SF. National Football League Players Have Higher Rates of Knee Extensor Mechanism Tears During Short and Normal Rest Weeks versus Long Rest Weeks. J Knee Surg. 2025. doi:10.1055/a-2585-5099
abstract: This retrospective observational study examined whether NFL quadriceps tendon and patellar tendon tears were associated with short, normal, or long rest weeks between games. Public sources including NFL injury reports, ESPN, and Pro-Football-Reference were used to identify regular-season extensor mechanism tears from 2009-2023, excluding preseason, postseason, practice, and week 1 injuries. Fifty-eight players met criteria. Injury rates did not differ between short and normal weeks, but players were more likely to sustain extensor mechanism tears during normal weeks and short weeks than during long weeks. Artificial turf was also associated with more overall extensor mechanism tears than natural grass.
tldr: Longer-than-normal rest appeared protective for NFL knee extensor mechanism tears; short weeks were not worse than normal weeks, but both carried higher rates than long weeks.
methods: The authors searched public NFL injury reports, ESPN, Pro-Football-Reference, NFL.com, and team sources for quadriceps or patellar tendon tears in NFL regular-season games from 2009-2023. Injuries after week 1 were classified by days since the player's previous game: short (<7 days), normal (7 days), or long (>7 days). Age, position, BMI, and playing surface were collected. Fisher's exact or chi-square tests, one-way ANOVA, and descriptive statistics were performed in Python.
findings: There were 58 extensor mechanism tears: 20 quadriceps tendon and 38 patellar tendon. Eight occurred in short weeks, 47 in normal weeks, and 3 in long weeks. Rates were 0.011 for short weeks, 0.015 for normal weeks, and 0.003 for long weeks. Normal weeks had 4.7 times higher risk than long weeks, and short weeks had 3.7 times higher risk than long weeks. Short and normal weeks did not differ. Artificial turf was associated with higher overall extensor mechanism tear occurrence.
limitations: The dataset came from publicly accessible reports, which can omit details or contain reporting errors. Days between games may not reflect each player's true recovery, practice load, rehabilitation exposure, travel, or readiness. The sample was small because extensor mechanism tears are rare and the inclusion criteria focused on regular-season game injuries.
practicalImplications: For Baylor football, treat extra recovery days as meaningful protection for severe knee tendon injury risk, especially around congested game stretches. Build additional tendon-load management, eccentric/isometric quadriceps capacity, monitoring, and recovery emphasis into short and normal weeks, and be attentive to surface exposure when planning practices and return progressions.
athleteDev: Linemen and other high-load athletes may need year-round quadriceps and patellar tendon capacity work, landing/deceleration quality, body-composition support, and individualized recovery plans. The surface finding supports tracking artificial-turf exposure as part of athlete workload history.
rtp: The study does not provide RTP criteria. It emphasizes that quadriceps and patellar tendon ruptures often require season-ending surgery, so RTP should be surgical-team led and based on tendon healing, strength symmetry, progressive football-specific loading, position demands, and tolerance across repeated high-force practices.
